text:
​ @genericperson360  

"Doesn’t matter how low the quality is, the point is that you made it..."

Then you would not be a true artist.

An artist strives to be the best they could be, to seek to make each bit of artwork better than their last. You don't settle for less, especially if you're going for realistic art (neoclassicism, romanticism) -- you either go big or you go home. You're not making insignificant doodles. You're creating something that has the chance to be remembered, to leave a profound impact, something that is profound.

Therefore, in order to create something of such a caliber, each work doesn't simply take talent or creativity (which, from what I've seen, seems to be the only thing that matters in the artistic world for some people), but it also takes time, funds, and energy. What happens, then, when you have little (if at all) of either of those last three? You get either delayed works, works of poor quality, works that are discarded mid-progress, or that don't come to fruition at all. A man could've been the best in his class in his artistic trade, but now, do you honestly expect that same man, who currently has depressive episodes, has pitiful funds to their name, with a nine-to-five, to create something of the same caliber as before?

So, yes...it's true that anybody can pick up a pencil and draw anything. Yet, very few (decreasing in number) are able to pick up a pencil and draw something phenomenal. People value creativity, as they should. It's a hallmark of civilization. Still, don't expect great works (emphasis on 'great') to appear exactly when you want them 24/7.

If someone really wants to create art, they WILL find a way to make it happen. You also forgot that art can just be made for fun and self fulfillment. Not every art piece is going to be some big masterpiece that changes the world, and not every piece NEEDS to be. Hell, most artists don’t even care about that. Art for many people is just a hobby they do for fun. Like i said before, literal toddlers can make art and enjoy themselves doing it, even if it’s stick figures drawn in crayon.
